content:
RAG Interview Question — PolicyBazaar (AI Engineer)

A policy doc gets updated this morning. But your assistant still serves the old answer.
The embedding was never refreshed... and a cached response is sitting there, stale.

The question:
How do you design index & cache invalidation so updated documents don't keep serving yesterday's answer?

This is where most RAG systems quietly break. Retrieval looks fine in the demo, then production starts lying to customers about coverage, premiums, and claim rules.

Here's how I'd answer 👇
✅ 1. Version every document
Each doc carries a content_hash + updated_at. On ingestion, compare hashes only re-embed chunks that actually changed (delta re-indexing), not the whole corpus. No silent drift.

✅ 2. Treat the vector index as mutable
On update: upsert new chunk vectors, then delete the old chunk IDs in the same transaction. Stale vectors lingering in the index are the #1 cause of "ghost" answers. Tie chunk IDs to doc_id:version so cleanup is deterministic.

✅ 3. Event-driven invalidation, not cron
A doc-update event (CDC / webhook / queue) triggers re-embedding immediately. Don't wait for a nightly batch — for insurance policy changes, "by tomorrow" is already wrong.

✅ 4. Make the cache version-aware
Cache key = hash(query) + knowledge_base_version. When a doc updates, bump the KB version → old cached answers become unreachable automatically. No manual flushing, no stale hits.

✅ 5. Safety net: TTL + verification
Short TTL on cached answers, and on cache hit, cheaply check whether the source chunks' versions still match. Mismatch → bypass cache, regenerate.

========================================
We’ve covered questions like these in my AI Engineering Interview Master Bundle, a comprehensive set of 22 courses designed for real interview prep.
Explore the full guide here → https://lnkd.in/gqFkWZd4
